facts_disclosed: Cart is on Soi Rangnam near Victory Monument BTS — same spot 4 years
Sells 200 plates minimum during lunch rush 11 to 1:30

turns.content: sooo I have a situation na ka. there is a NEW khao man gai cart that set up near my spot and I am a little bit worried 😤 like is there room for two of us??
Ooh, competition moving in! That can be stressful. How close are they to your spot, and how long have you been set up there? A little context will help me give you a better take on the situation.

okay so my cart is on Soi Rangnam na ka. near Victory Monument BTS. I been at this SAME spot for 4 years!! 4 years I build my customer base here. the office workers around there know me, they come every day for lunch. and now this new cart show up like 50 meters from me selling the same thing

Four years in the same spot is a serious advantage — that's not just a location, that's a reputation built over time. The office workers who come daily aren't going to switch overnight just because a new cart appeared 50 meters away. You've got loyalty on your side.

That said, 50 meters is pretty close for the same dish. Have you seen how busy they are? And is their pricing similar to yours?

I walk past yesterday to look haha. very sneaky. they sell at 45 baht!! 5 baht cheaper than me. and the portion look okay but honestly the rice look dry and they use gas stove. but still 45 baht will attract some people right?? my lunch rush is 11 to 1:30 and I sell 200 plates minimum in that window. I don't want to lose even 10 plates to this person

200 plates in a 2.5-hour lunch rush — that's insane volume and it means you've got a machine running smoothly. Losing a few plates to a 45-baht competitor would sting, but let me put this in perspective:

You have several advantages they don't:
- **4 years of regulars** who know your name and your food
- **Charcoal-cooked chicken** vs their gas stove — your regulars will taste the difference
- **Consistent quality** that comes from experience
- **Your reputation** — you're a known name in the area, not just a random cart

The 5-baht difference might pull in some price-sensitive people, but most of your loyal lunch crowd comes specifically for YOUR khao man gai. Do the office workers who eat with you daily seem like the type to switch over 5 baht?

haha no actually. I have this one customer P'Lek who come every single day for 3 years. he will never switch. and the construction guys from the site nearby they always come to me. I think you are right — the loyal ones will stay. but what about the NEW people who walk by and see 45 baht vs 50 baht??

That's a fair concern — new customers who don't know either of you yet will compare. Here are some things you can do without lowering your price:

1. **Make your cart more visible** — better signage, maybe photos of your food displayed prominently. First impressions matter for walk-by traffic.
2. **Offer a small extra** — a cup of the chicken broth on the side is traditional for khao man gai. If you already do that, make sure it's generous. It adds perceived value.
3. **Let your regulars spread the word** — happy customers talk. Word of mouth near Victory Monument will keep bringing people TO you specifically.
4. **Let quality speak** — anyone who tries both will come back to you if yours is genuinely better.

Do NOT lower your price to match them. That's a race to the bottom. Your food is worth 50 baht.

okay good because I was thinking about dropping to 45 baht and I am glad you say no. 50 baht is fair for my quality na ka. and yes I already give free broth with every plate!! big cup. the broth is from the same pot I cook the chicken so it is so flavourful. some customer come JUST for the broth haha. they say it cure their hangover 😂

The broth is a huge selling point! That's something you should highlight more — it's part of the complete experience. Hangover cure broth is honestly great word-of-mouth marketing too.

And absolutely do not drop to 45 baht. If you sell 200 plates at 50 baht, that's 10,000 baht. At 45 baht, that's 9,000 baht — you'd lose 1,000 baht per day just in the lunch rush, which is 26,000 baht per month. All because of one new cart? Not worth it.

arai wa 26,000 baht per month!! when you put it like that absolutely not. okay I keep my price. honestly the spot on Soi Rangnam is everything in street food. the foot traffic near Victory Monument BTS is crazy. there is enough people for both of us I think. I just got scared for a moment

That's the right attitude. Victory Monument area has massive foot traffic — there are literally thousands of office workers, students, and commuters passing through every day. There's absolutely room for two khao man gai carts, especially when yours has the established reputation.

Honestly, in Thai street food culture, competition nearby can even help. People see a cluster of food carts and it becomes a food destination. They might try the new person once and then discover you're better. Think of it as them bringing more attention to the area, not stealing your customers.

you know what that is actually a Thai way of thinking. like Chinatown have 100 noodle shop but they all survive because people go there FOR noodle. okay I feel better na ka. I will focus on my food and my customers and not worry about this new cart. if my khao man gai is good — and it IS good — they cannot touch me 💪 okay motivation is back. sabai sabai. I go prepare for tomorrow now. the charcoal is not going to light itself haha 🔥 thank you na ka 🙏 goodnight!!

That's exactly the right mindset! Yaowarat is a perfect example — all those noodle shops side by side and they're all packed because the cluster creates demand. You've got 4 years, 200 plates a day, charcoal flavour, and regulars who won't leave you. That new cart should be worried about YOU, not the other way around.

Go prep, get some sleep, and crush the lunch rush tomorrow. Your khao man gai earned that spot on Soi Rangnam. Goodnight! 🙏💪
